Thanks very much! Looks like an interesting tool and we really appreciate the effort you've put in so far on supporting LightGBM.

NOTE: I changed the title of this pull request to be more descriptive of the change. I did this because PR titles become changelog entries in LightGBM releases (e.g. see https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/releases/tag/v3.2.1), but in general I think you'll find that open source projects would prefer that your PR titles be descriptive and not just branch names.

I read through https://neptune.ai/blog/lightgbm-parameters-guide and have a few minor notes I'd like you to consider (not necessary for merging this PR though!).

* https://github.com/huanzhang12/lightgbm-gpu is not actively maintained and has not been updated in 4+ years, so it can't be relied on as an accurate documentation of the current state of LightGBM. You might @szilard and @Laurae2 's work in https://github.com/szilard/GBM-perf a more accurate benchmark of LightGBM's performance with different settings and hardware.
* remove code samples that use filepaths specific to a one person's local filesystem, like `neptune.init('mjbahmani/LightGBM-hyperparameters')`

Thanks - this looks great. We should keep backwards compatibility with dicts or at least raise an actionable DeprecationWarning.

The conversion back to a scalingconfig data class is too specific to be included in Ray Tune's resolve logic. In my opinion there are two possible solutions here:

1. The scaling config is converted to a dict before passing to Tune, and back to a data class in the trainer before calling the self.training_loop. This would be in-line with the current logic, and the specific `scaling_config` key is in-scope of the Ray AIR trainer
2. Alternatively, we can introduce a `tune.sample.TuneableDataclass` abstraction and have `ScalingConfig` inherit from this. Ray Tune would then check for these classes in the original spec, convert them into dicts, and convert them back into their respective dataclasses afterwards. This is more involved and possibly out of scope of this PR.

I'm happy with either solution but believe that 1) is less invasive and can be done quickly.

I've been reviewing this for the last couple of days. Very clever all around. Amazing job, as always! 🚀

I added a couple of comments here and there. A couple of opinions in the places where I suspect my opinion could be relevant.

## Type Checks from VS Code - PyRight

I noticed a couple of errors with VS Code/PyRight in some places, I think mainly about getting a dict key form a dict where it is not required, or things like that.

Would it be valuable to point them out and suggest the fixes or is it just too much noise on top of the actual PR review?

I'm just adding a couple of those comments for now.

## Questions

About some of the questions in the description, my opinions inline.

Pretty much in favor with everything, for the rest I don't have a strong opinion or enough knowledge.

**Things left to resolve:**

* [x] Field kwargs
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'examples' kwarg on `Field`?
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Re-add the examples keyword argument
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. All things equal, +1 to re-adding it. Unless there's something against it.
	* [x] Should we remove or keep the 'const' kwarg on `Field`? (This was used to indicate a field was required and the only allowed value was its default.)
		* If not, what is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop this argument in favor of `Literal` and/or validators as appropriate (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. If it's the same, I would think it's better to keep one single way to do it, and if there's now a "standard" way to do it with type annotations, I would use that. One single way reduces combinatorial logic, maintenance, testing. And it's easier for users to learn one single thing than two, even more if it's two competing ones.
	* [x] Should we change the `regex` kwarg to `pattern` in the `Field` signature?
		* **Tentative plan:** Change `regex` to `pattern` (i.e., keep as is on main branch)
			* [@tiangolo]: In favor with plan. As JSON Schema uses the same `pattern` keyword, I would vote for that. `regex` might be more intuitive from Python-land, but for me, `regex` is more ambiguous as to if it takes a regular expression object or a pattern string. Even though the Python docs also refer to a Pattern object, but anyway, I personally associate the word "pattern" more easily with the string.
	* [x] Do we want to do a backwards-compatibility handler thing with `**kwargs` — warning about now-invalid kwargs (whether or not we keep them working) on `Field`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Do not attempt to maintain backwards compatibility, but document in the migration guide
			* [@tiangolo] **Strongly** in favor with plan. More details below, and also inline on the PR.


* [x] ConfigDict fields
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'schema_extra' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* Either way, do we retain/drop support on a config _class_ in the model definition?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.
	* [x] Do we want to re-add 'fields' as a field on model_config?
		* If not, should we drop the tests?
		* What is the migration path?
		* **Tentative plan:** Drop in favor of annotations and/or `= Field(...)`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan.

* [ ] Default values
	* If a default value of a field is not JSON encodable, do we include anything in the schema indicating the default value? Do we error?
	* **Tentative plan:** Just don't include the value in the schema if json encoding fails, but keep the `required` field set appropriately. I believe this is perfectly compatible with JSON schema.
		* [@tiangolo] Soft in favor with plan. I'm not sure about the implications, but I think it should be fine.

* [ ] `__modify_schema__` (-> `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`)
	* Do we retain backwards compatibility with the name `__modify_schema__`, or force migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`?
		* **Tentative plan:** Force the migration to `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__`
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. This is the moment to break changes.
	* Do we support any args/kwargs for `__pydantic_modify_json_schema__` besides the schema itself?
		* Do we pass any info to it from FieldInfo? The CoreSchema? A "source" object? (Could be useful when working with custom generics)
		* **Tentative plan:** Not sure. Would potentially be useful to have access to the core schema and to the type "source" (e.g., to check generic parameter values, etc.)

* [ ] FastAPI
	* Do we need to retain functions analogous to any of the following? (Note we have retained `model_schema`, and `schema`.)
		* get_flat_models_from_field/get_flat_models_from_fields
		* get_flat_models_from_model/get_flat_models_from_models
		* get_model_name_map
		* model_process_schema
	* If not, can we drop their tests?
	* **Tentative plan:** Could we instead build a CoreSchema in FastAPI that produces the desired JSON schema?
		* I'm inclined to put significant effort into helping with the migration of FastAPI to pydantic v2, and to implement any necessary analogous functions as a part of that effort. But if it's already clear that we will need them, happy to add them now.
			* [@tiangolo] In favor with plan. And thanks, as always! 🎉 Better not to add premature optimizations/abstractions. And more details in line in the PR.

* [ ] Callable type
	* How should we handle JSON schema generation for fields with type callable? (See, e.g., `tests.test_schema.test_callable_type`)
	* **Tentative plan:** Not sure.

* [ ] Decimal
	* What should the Decimal schema be?
	* **Tentative plan:** Need to come to a consensus; https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/issues/1511 may be relevant
		* Note: although serialization and parsing are different, I think for FastAPI/OpenAPI purposes it is important that the JSON schema be compatible with the serialization type. (Or else generated clients will produce type errors if an API returns a Decimal.) Happy to discuss this more.
			* [@tiangolo] agreed that incompatible clients would be a non-obvious but important problem. Although I'm not sure I understand the problem well and what's the "serialization type" in this context.

## kwargs in `Field()`

Details about asking for no more kwargs in `Field()`:

I would request to **not** accept any extra kwargs. I asked for that here in this hidden comment: https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic/pull/4516#discussion_r1010870226

Copying here for context/completeness:

> This `**extra` has been used to add stuff to JSON Schema.

> But in some cases, people pass other stuff here expecting it to be used later or for other purposes, but then it also affects JSON Schema.

> I would like to ask to make it a single keyword argument `extra` instead of `**extra`, or alternatively, to have another single argument `json_schema_extra` so that extensions for JSON Schema can be apart from extra values passed here for other purposes.

> I haven't seen a use for this apart from JSON Schema, and the fact that it makes `Field()` accept any keyword arguments makes editors not complain about arguments that actually do nothing. I've seen this sometimes, e.g. recently with a `Field(required=True)`, but `required` is not a valid argument, if it's required or not is defined by the absence of a `default`. But the code seemed fine and linters passed, because it was taken by the catch all `**extra`.

> It's a breaking change, but I think it's totally worth it and this is the right time for those changes.

## Semi-off topic, highlights

**Note**: no action item here, just praise and highlights of non-obvious extra benefits. 🎉

Very clever implementation! 🚀

I like how the core schema refs, def refs, and json refs are stored and correlated, and then updated when necessary, but still stored. And how everything is stored in the object.

I think this will allow me to do something I've been wanting to do in FastAPI, to pre-generate the JSON Schema stuff for each endpoint, and when there's an error, to show the specific endpoint with an error instead of just saying "something is wrong".

## Wishlist - collect errors

I was checking the wishlist of things I wanted for Pydantic and almost everything is already done. 🎉

From the things not there yet, there's one single thing I would like to ask that is related to JSON Schema, although not sure if it could be a subsequent PR. It's also related to the point above.

### The problem

First the problem.

Currently, an example like this, rightfully breaks:

```Pyhton
from pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict


class Car:
    def __init__(self, make: str, model: str, year: int):
        self.make = make
        self.model = model
        self.year = year

class Model(BaseModel):
    f1: int = 1
    f2: Car

    model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True)


Model.model_json_schema()
```

But if `Model` is in a FastAPI path operation, and maybe `Car` is in a sub-model in a sub-field, in a large app, when calling the OpenAPI endpoint the whole thing will break, saying there's a problem with `Car`, but without saying where exactly... and more importantly, none of the OpenAPI will be generated. So, a single broken field breaks the whole OpenAPI and Swagger UI.

### Feature Request

Currently it immediately raises an error like:

```
Cannot generate a JsonSchema for X
```

...that stops the rest of the JSON Schema generation.

I would like instead to accumulate those errors and get them all together, but to be able to retrieve the rest of the JSON Schema.

### Possible Implementation

I think it's better to state the problem in case you see a better way to solve it, but I can also imagine possible implementations. For example:

```Python
schema = Model.model_json_schema()
```

...raises as normally, but:

```Python
schema, errors = Model.model_json_schema(keep_errors=True)
```

Doesn't raise, and returns a JSON Schema where anything with an error is replaced with `{}` and `errors` is a list of stored exceptions. Of course, `keep_errors` could use some bikeshedding on the parameter name, the same for the return interface, but you get the idea.

It could also be a context manager, maybe too complex, but that would keep the API interface (params, return) of `model_json_schema()` unmodified for both cases, don't know. And it would probably only be used by FastAPI and libraries like that, so not too bad on that side. But it would require contextvars and all that dance. E.g.

```Python
with ErrorCollector as c:
	schema = Model.model_json_schema()
print(e.errors)
```

Again, the specific implementation is up for discussion, but I would like to have a way to do this.

### Plan in FastAPI

My plan is also later to put this in FastAPI in a way that (if I manage to) when there's an error like this, the traceback would use Rich and point to the right file and line with the specific FastAPI *path operation* that uses that model. But I think that to be able to do that I first need to pre-compute and collect the JSON Schema errors per path-operation, and not only at the global level.

## Breaking Changes

About the breaking changes, mostly renaming things, in the small few places this happens, I think this is the right moment to do it and discard the cruft of backwards compatibility for those things.

An improvement in that migration, alternative to keep backwards compatibility, would be a tool to automatically update code to use the new things, for the simple ones (I can imagine, e.g. moving kwargs in `Field()` to `json_schema_extra`, or changing `regex` to `pattern`). Maybe that could also work for the `model_config`.

I haven't done it, but I know it's possible and feasible with LibCST. @Kludex has built a couple of CLI tools that do that type of thing, e.g. https://github.com/Kludex/bump-testclient

The other requirement here is to find a way to avoid calling other methods on the provider when its setup() hasn't succeeded.  For example, we need to prevent calls from `provision(..)` on the aws provider if `setup` didn't succeed, as otherwise `this.region` and `this.s3` might not be set correctly.

Some comments

An annoying question: is this something that should be in pybind11, rather than say ... an external plugin `pybind11_filesystem`? I was first skeptical about this, but the actual implementation is rather clean/small, so I guess I'm OK with this.
I'd like to see pybind11 evolve to a more "plugin-based" architecture, but I guess this specific feature isn't a huge burden to maintain?

I wanted to suggest thinking about `pathlib2`, for Python 2 (and PyPy 2, which isn't deprecated), but PyPy doesn't work, currently, ofc. And in Python 2 we still lack `os.PathLike` and `__fspath__`?

Next question: does this also accept strings? If not, should strings be accepted, maybe if `convert == true` in `load`? If they already are, a test on this would be nice.

Finally, a request: could this be turned into an abstraction like `optional_caster`? Basically, this would rename the current caster, and just have `template <> struct type_caster<std::filesystem::path> : public path_caster<std::filesystem::path> {}`.

This would allow for other C++ path-like type to quickly opt-in to this behavior, if they support a minimal interface (be constructible/assignable from `std::string` and having `.native()` or so).

Thanks for the contribution @Charlottez112! You'll want to link this PR to #578, although depending on exactly what functions eventually get modified by this PR it may not be sufficient to finish all of #578.

The reason for the current failure of the tests is an unfortunate idiosyncrasy of Cython. You have put a `cdef` inside an `if` statement, which is not allowed in Cython (happy to chat more about why offline if you'd like an extended explanation). The appropriate fix is to replace code that looks like this:

```
if CONDITION:
    cdef int x = 1
else:
    cdef int x = 2
```
with something like this:
```
cdef int x
if CONDITION:
    x = 1
else:
    x = 2
```

You should also write new tests for the inplace behavior. Have a look at some of the existing tests for box wrapping in `tests/test_box_Box.py` and try to modify the box wrapping one to use your inplace argument. Your test should verify that arrays are actually modified in place.

something like this:
```python
def log_invocation(fn):
    @functools.wraps(fn)
    def log_wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        ctx = click.get_current_context()
        print(ctx.command.name)
        print(ctx.params)
        return fn(*args, **kwargs)

    return log_wrapper
```

@log_invocation

sorry, but I do not really understand what pain point this is trying to address. the container is not built from source, and it is developing in an arm64 linux container (which runs inside a linux vm), which is an environment that no one really uses today (and we also have almost no tests running on arm64 linux platforms).

note that, the requirements and dependencies are checked in inside the repo today, and gets updated like every week by people. so if the dev container here is not able to pick the dependencies from the repo, I do not think it can really claim that "this provides a hermetic environment with all needed dependencies.".

also `ray-deps` do not have ml dependencies, it is just a `base-deps` with a wheel copied in. so the use seems to be a bit limited.

I feel that one can just create an arm64 graviton linux ec2 vm (or x86), and achieve the approximately same thing with vscode+ssh, and even without containers (which is basically the workflow I uses today).

maybe it makes linux local dev easier, but I worry about the false signals that it might provide. because the platform it uses is the least used one.

constructively, here are some points for improvement:

- if the purpose is to test with the dependencies, to make this useful, I think this should be building from source, ideally without adding new `Dockerfile` that is only used for dev.
- ray supposedly also supports osx and apple silicon, so if the purpose is to test ray library logic, instead of using a container, maybe we should get the apple silicon native dev environment work properly?
- for getting the dependencies, the requirements are also declared in `python/requirements.txt`. maybe just use that, rather than using `ray-deps`?

I'm fine with the added functionality, but I want to note the following caveats:

- Users can still get `MemcachedUnexpectedCloseError`, even with this change. This is a distributed system, so it will be very easy for the timing to work out such that the connection looks "live" on the client side, but has become "idle" on the server side.
- Users still need to expect and handle `MemcacheUnexpectedCloseError`. I'm starting to think, after recent discussions, that we need to provide more guidance for how to do this. There is no way to guarantee that you will never get this exception.

With those caveats, I think this can eliminate some spurious cases of `MemcacheUnexpectedCloseError`, which should make normal error handling a little more stream-lined.

Thanks! Yamllint support sounds great :)

Thoughts on not adding a target type for this, and instead having it operate on any YAML file? We have less precedent for that because the technology is newer, but it's a direction we're interesting in moving more towards. 

My main concern with targets is the intersection with `files` vs `resources` -- usually you'd use those targets to describe YAML files and add them as `dependencies` to your other targets like `python_sources`. That's going to be necessary to keep doing, and the backends like Python won't do anything when you add a `yaml_source` target to their `dependencies` because they only add `file` and `resource`.

Another benefit of not using targets is it's less boilerplate.

The only thing we don't have totally figured out is how to handle telling Pants to skip linting certain YAML files. Normally, we have the `skip_my_tool` field on individual targets, like `skip_flake8`. That can be nice because it's decentralized. Here, the only idea I have is the option `[yammlint].skip_globs`. It should work out well, only is a difference that it's now a centralized list rather than decentralized in BUILD files.

What do you think? 

Here's an example of a file-less linter, and I'm happy to give better instructions if you agree with this path:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/afd41699a791208b785a893e1687950b2a0f2f36/src/python/pants/backend/project_info/regex_lint.py#L258-L342

A couple of concerns:

1) I think the PR description is missing an explanation of the actual problem(s) that the library currently has with uWSGI which would provide a lot more context to the changes (which may render my next concern moot).

2) As far as I can tell, `ddtrace-run` is only broken with uWSGI when threads are spawned in the master process. This occurs only when 1) run-time metrics are enabled since the thread is spawned immediately; or 2) when the AgentWriter thread starts (only happens once a trace is written). Since runtime metrics are not publicly documented (private beta) and it's unlikely that traces are written from the master process(?) it seems like uWSGI + `ddtrace-run` would work in almost all use-cases at the moment (unless I'm missing something).

It seems a bit drastic to me to introduce breaking functionality by making `ddtrace-run` incompatible with uWSGI entirely. It seems like we could do better by checking if runtime metrics are enabled and used under uWSGI and warning/crashing only then.

If we want to create a semver package, it is probably better to use a src-layout. Although there is some disagreement about that, I've read [Ionel's](https://blog.ionelmc.ro/2017/09/25/rehashing-the-src-layout/) and [Hynek's](https://hynek.me/articles/testing-packaging/#src) blog posts and think it has some value.

That said, I would suggest to use this structure in our root folder:

```
src/
└── semver
    ├── cli.py
    ├── deprecated.py
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── __main__.py
    ├── _types.py
    └── version.py
```

Apart from that, I've commented on some parts. These are some ideas and I'd like to hear your opinion.

Another question: Did you pull from the upstream project? It looks like there are some old commits there. Not exactly sure how you set up your branch. I would recommend to try to be in sync with the upstream master branch first, otherwise it make things much harder later.

In particular, I think the changelog, docs, and tests should be at least included in the `sdist` (see https://python-poetry.org/docs/pyproject/#packages). For cryptography, I'd like to hear how you recommend migrating them to the new system. I think ideally we'd create one PR that would land before this is merged to stop things from breaking and then create another PR that'd use the `poetry.lock` file.

> I did not do the thing where I loop over two iterators running the same code for each, since what happens when running each offsetIterator is quite different. I've put in a block comment describing the two passes, for your enjoyment.

Fair enough, was just an idea. Now that this PR's diff is smaller, it looks good. I took two minutes and found that my idea doesn't end up much saving much net code, and your version is more explicit.

<details>
<summary> my attempt</summary>

```diff
diff --git a/music21/musicxml/m21ToXml.py b/music21/musicxml/m21ToXml.py
index d71a4479e..3f6d34e42 100644
--- a/music21/musicxml/m21ToXml.py
+++ b/music21/musicxml/m21ToXml.py
@@ -3288,56 +3288,65 @@ class MeasureExporter(XMLExporterBase):
         # beginning and does all the prePostObjectSpanners processing.
         # If there are no SpannerAnchors, we do one pass, doing any prePostObjectSpanners
         # processing as we process each element.
+        objIteratorSpannerAnchors: OffsetIterator[base.Music21Object] = OffsetIterator(
+            m).addFilter(lambda el: self._hasPrePostObjectSpanners(el))
+        objIteratorAllElse: OffsetIterator[base.Music21Object] = OffsetIterator(
+            m).addFilter(lambda el: not self._hasPrePostObjectSpanners(el))
+        for passNumber, objIterator in enumerate(objIteratorSpannerAnchors, objIteratorAllElse):
+            firstPass = passNumber == 0
+            for objGroup in objIterator:
+                hasSpannerAnchors: bool = bool(m[spanner.SpannerAnchor])
+
+                # First (and possibly only) pass:
+                # Group all objects by offsets and then do a different order than normal sort.
+                # That way chord symbols and other 0-width objects appear before notes as much as
+                # possible.
+
+                # Second pass: If this flat stream (measure/voice) contains any SpannerAnchors, we
+                # will perform a second pass, emitting any pre/postList for any object that is
+                # functioning as a spanner anchor (the SpannerAnchors, as well as any GeneralNotes
+                # that start or end spanners).
+                objGroup: list[base.Music21Object]
+                objIterator: OffsetIterator[base.Music21Object] = OffsetIterator(m)
+                for objGroup in objIterator:
+                    groupOffset = m.elementOffset(objGroup[0])
+                    offsetToMoveForward = groupOffset - self.offsetInMeasure
+                    if offsetToMoveForward > 0:
+                        if not firstPass or any(
